---
title: Logic
description: Propositions, truth values, and implication.
---

::: {.learning-objectives}
### Learning Objectives

- Work with true/false propositions.
- Interpret logical implication.
- Generate simple truth tables.
:::

```{python}
from edumath.discrete_math.logic import implies, truth_table

truth_table(("p", "q"), implies)
```

::: {.practice-box}
1. When is `p -> q` false?
2. What does a truth table show?
3. Why is logic useful for proofs and programming?
:::

## Solutions

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## Solution 1

Answer: when `p` is true and `q` is false.

That is the only case where an implication fails.
:::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## Solution 2

Answer: every combination of truth values for the inputs and the resulting
truth value of the expression.
:::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## Solution 3

Answer: logic makes assumptions, conditions, implications, and correctness
precise.
:::
